context: English has one word for "to be". Spanish has two - ser and estar - and they are not interchangeable. The good news: you already use both. "Soy de Noruega" is ser; "estoy bien" is estar. This lesson just makes the choice conscious.
explanation: Use soy (from ser) for who you are and where you're from - the things that don't change: "soy turista", "soy de Noruega". Use estoy (from estar) for how you are and where you are right now - things that can change: "estoy bien", "estoy aqui". The "he/she/it" form of ser is es ("es un cafe"). A quick test: identity or origin -> ser; a state or a location -> estar.
